text: We start by dissecting the inferior pulmonary ligament with the Maryland bipolar forceps in one and the CADEAR forceps in two.
We begin the lymphadenectomy with the station R9 lymph node.
which is then subsequently removed by the bedside assistant using a Johan.
We now go back to the division of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
As a result of this, we can see the exposure of some further lymph nodes.
The one in current view, most likely being a further R9 lymph node.
We are now dissecting the pleura up posteriorly behind the hilum of the lung, just grasping anteriorly to the vagus nerve.
Here you can see the removal of the R10 lymph node as we dissect it off the vagus nerve.
This is achieved through the use of the bipolar energy set to 70 watts on the Maryland forceps.
This being the maximum that can be set through the delivery device.
We continue to divide the lymph node off the back of the right atrium.
Here just reflecting the lymph node forwards and continuing to dissect some of the pleura.
And now you can see the removal of the R10 lymph node by the bedside assistant.
We now go a little bit deeper for the removal of another R10 lymph node.
Just a bit higher than this will be a station 7 lymph node, which would become a station 10 when it is below the level of the bronchus intermedius.
Here we are dividing the pleura higher up over the right main bronchus.
As we move ahead you can see the azygous at the top of the field as we continue to divide this thicker pleura off the right main bronchus.
We are now going under the azygous dissecting the pleura towards the oesophagus.
Now we are going more peripherally over the right upper lobe bronchus.
Here you can see the removal of an upper lobe R10 lymph node, which is being taken off the right upper lobe bronchus.
And again, as you can see, being removed by the bedside assistant through the use of a Johan.
We've now gone into the fissure with the lower lobe visible at the bottom of the frame and the upper lobe with adhesions at the top.
As we continue to divide these adhesions off the lower lobe, you can see at the far right of the screen, the middle lobe has come into view.

When doing a segmentectomy, it's important to expose and visualize the pulmonary artery within the fissure, particularly when doing a posterior segmentectomy, as you need to be able to visualize the posterior ascending artery.
Having divided the adhesions, we are now dividing the pleura over the pulmonary artery.
and just below the maryland you can see the basal pulmonary artery.
Here we are tunnelling posteriorly just over the pulmonary artery and also the segment 6 artery of which supplies the apical segment of the right lower lobe.
Having passed the Maryland posteriorly, here we are using a sling which can help guide the stapler in order to complete the posterior oblique fissure.
A sling doesn't necessarily have to always be used as a gold curved tip stapler with a leader cath can also do the same job.
Here you can see the use of a purple 60 signia powered tri stapler.
Here you can see the bedside assistant just being guided by the surgeon
And here, just using the instruments to pull the lung tissue down into the jaws of the stapler as the bedside assistant fires it.
As you can see, the stapler is moving quite slowly.
This is because of the thickness of the tissue.
The Signia stapler decides this rate accordingly.
As you can see, this was completed in one firing with just a tiny bit requiring division.
So now we can see the basal pulmonary artery at the bottom and what we are dividing here is the right upper lobe posterior segmental vein.
Here you can see the identification of an R11 lymph node which is located between the upper and lower lobe and most likely sat on the bronchus.
It is important to remove all of these lymph nodes as it allows for good exposure of the anatomy, particularly the bronchus and the vessels.
And finally the removal of this interlobar R11 lymph node.
Here we have a much better view of the posterior segmental vein and just under the maryland is the right upper lobe vein proper.
And now just holding the posterior segmental vein, behind which we can see the posterior ascending artery with quite an early bifurcation.
Firstly, we need to divide this posterior segmental vein, which the bipolar energy of the Maryland forceps is used for this small peripheral branch.
It was thought that this posterior segmental vein was too small for the use of a stapler.
A real advantage of robotics is the ability to knot tie.
As a result, this was utilised for this small posterior segmental vein using a silk tie.
Following the completion of a number of knots, the bipolar energy of the Maryland forceps was used to complete the division of this posterior segmental vein.
Coming back to the posterior ascending artery, the early bifurcation led to questions as to whether the anterior bifurcation was an aberrant S3 or an anterior segmental artery.
As a result, the posterior bifurcation was divided as this was definitely going to the posterior segment.
To check this, ICG is used in order to find which of the blood supply had been cut and which hadn't.
Now we are just completing the division of the posterior bifurcation through the use of a Tan 45 with a gold tip and leader cath.
This allowed for a good view of the posterior segmental bronchus.
This can be very easily checked as it can be clamped by the cadia and then inflated.
Here you can see the bronchus being clamped by the cadia at the bottom left of the screen.
Subsequent inflation confirms that that is the posterior segmental bronchus and not the whole upper low bronchus.
Following this confirmation, a stapler with a leader cath is passed under the posterior segmental bronchus.
Sometimes it may be easier to close it off using the cadia as sometimes the presence of the relatively bulky stapler can occlude other bronchi.
The use of a leader cath allows the surgeon to pull the bedside assistant into the right place
Here we are using a tan load stapler.
Usually a purple would be used for bronchi.
However, due to this being a segmental bronchi, a tan load is sufficient.
We now go back to the pulmonary arteries and the use of indeciding green.
As this is injected, it allows the surgeon to visualize which areas are still vascularized.
What was found in this case is that the back of the posterior segment was not vascularized.
However, as you can see, this area currently in view still has a good blood supply.
This indicating that the anterior bifurcation of that posterior ascending artery requires division.
We now begin the dissection of the station 2R and 4R lymph nodes located just above the azygous.
It is good to do a complete lymphadenectomy of these lymph nodes following the SVC up until you come to the vagus nerve.
It is important to also take out the fatty tissue.
As you can see here, we are removing an R4 lymph node.
We now come back to the residual posterior ascending artery of which using the ICG was confirmed to still be supplying a section of the posterior segment.
and here we can see its division through the use of a Tan45 stapler.
Just continuing the lymphadenectomy, just behind the right upper lobe vein and on the right upper lobe bronchus is an R12 lymph node, which is a lobar lymph node.
Just continuing the R12 lymphadenectomy.
These lymph nodes will be sent for frozen section in the hope that they come back negative.
However, if they do show that they are positive, a completion lobectomy should be performed.
Now we repeat the ICG in order to confirm that the correct vasculature has been divided and therefore that the posterior segment no longer has a blood supply.
Here you can just see the stumps of the artery vein and bronchus.
We then proceed to staple across the area of devascularization of which was shown by the ICG and also the aeration of the bronchial test.
Here we are using a purple 60 and again it is going quite slowly due to the thickness of the tissue.
It is important to get the stapler under the stumps as we continue the posterior segmental resection, of which has advanced just slightly into the apical segment.
Here we are just orientating the sample in order to divide across the line of aeration versus deaeration, which was also confirmed through the ICG.
And here we come to the final division, which will complete the posterior segmentectomy.
